title: Development of the middle class
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on the development of the middle class.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Line graph showing the number of Clerical Workers by Sex, from 1870 to 1900. The years 1870, 1880, 1890, and 1900 are indicated along the horizontal axis. The vertical axis is the number of workers in increments of 200,000 starting with 0 and ending with 600,000. There are two lines on the graph, one showing the number of male clerical workers and one showing the number of female clerical workers. Both lines move generally upward. In 1870, there were  79,709 male clerical workers and 1,910 female clerical workers. In 1880, there were 153,440 male clerical workers and 7, 040 female clerical workers. In 1890 there were 391,526 male clerical workers and 77,060 female clerical workers. And in 1900, there were 550,433 male clerical workers and 187,053 female clerical workers.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6710d5c36e288145aea36ec8b73d728.svg)


Source: Comparative Occupation Statistics, 1870-1930, [Census Bureau,](https://www2.census.gov/library/publications/decennial/1940/population-occupation/00312147ch2.pdf) 

**The main trend shown in the graph was most directly associated with which of the following processes occurring in the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growth of a distinctive middle class
the concentration of wealth
the urbanization of American society
the rise in power of labor unions

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the graph and answer the question below. 

![Line graph showing the number of Clerical Workers by Sex, from 1870 to 1900. The years 1870, 1880, 1890, and 1900 are indicated along the horizontal axis. The vertical axis is the number of workers in increments of 200,000 starting with 0 and ending with 600,000. There are two lines on the graph, one showing the number of male clerical workers and one showing the number of female clerical workers. Both lines move generally upward. In 1870, there were  79,709 male clerical workers and 1,910 female clerical workers. In 1880, there were 153,440 male clerical workers and 7, 040 female clerical workers. In 1890 there were 391,526 male clerical workers and 77,060 female clerical workers. And in 1900, there were 550,433 male clerical workers and 187,053 female clerical workers.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6710d5c36e288145aea36ec8b73d728.svg)

Source: Comparative Occupation Statistics, 1870-1930, [Census Bureau,](https://www2.census.gov/library/publications/decennial/1940/population-occupation/00312147ch2.pdf) 

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the overall trend depicted in the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: improvements in business organization
increases in families’ leisure time
innovations in labor-saving technology
social justice efforts by voluntary organizations

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below.

|| **Bachelor's degree recipients, 1869-1900** ||
Gender | 1869-1870 | 1879-1880 | 1889-1890 | 1899-1900
- | - | - | - | -
Male | 7,993 | 10,411 | 12,857 | 22,173
Female |1,378 | 2,485 | 2,682 | 5,237

Source: [Department of Education](https://nces.ed.gov/pubs93/93442.pdf), 1993

**The major trend depicted in the graph most directly reflects which of the following developments in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of a middle class as the United States industrialized
the lack of job opportunities in the manufacturing industry
the effects of the extension of suffrage to women
the consequences of philanthropy on educational attainment

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the table and answer the question below.

|| **Bachelor's degree recipients, 1869-1900** ||
Gender | 1869-1870 | 1879-1880 | 1889-1890 | 1899-1900
- | - | - | - | -
Male | 7,993 | 10,411 | 12,857 | 22,173
Female |1,378 | 2,485 | 2,682 | 5,237

Source: [Department of Education](https://nces.ed.gov/pubs93/93442.pdf), 1993

**The main trend shown in the graph was most directly associated with which of the following processes occurring in the late nineteenth century?*

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the improvements in Americans’ standard of living
the changes in gender roles for women
the emergence of Jim Crow segregation
the urbanization of American society

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“In the fall of 1873, Yale and Princeton met in the first game of their long football rivalry. The players had no uniforms or equipment as we think of them now. They played a soccerlike game under rules agreed on just one month earlier. . . . 

>“Twenty years later, teams throughout the country were playing a distinctly American game of football under a standard code of rules, with standard formations and tactics. They also had plenty of footballs and special football uniforms, made in America and clearly products of a prospering sporting goods industry — a network of manufacturers, distributors, and retailers that had meshed in the late 1860s as baseball became the national pastime. . . . 

>“These ‘new providers’ determined much of the leisure revolution that turned informal pastimes into commodities.”

>-Source: Richard Butsch, historian, *For Fun and Profit: The Transformation of Leisure Into Consumption,* 1990

**The excerpt best illustrates which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in a consumer culture as leisure time grew
the development of an American national culture
the impact of industrialization on the production of goods
the emergence of new higher education opportunities

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“In the fall of 1873, Yale and Princeton met in the first game of their long football rivalry. The players had no uniforms or equipment as we think of them now. They played a soccerlike game under rules agreed on just one month earlier. . . . 

>“Twenty years later, teams throughout the country were playing a distinctly American game of football under a standard code of rules, with standard formations and tactics. They also had plenty of footballs and special football uniforms, made in America and clearly products of a prospering sporting goods industry — a network of manufacturers, distributors, and retailers that had meshed in the late 1860s as baseball became the national pastime. . . . 

>“These ‘new providers’ determined much of the leisure revolution that turned informal pastimes into commodities.”

>-Source: Richard Butsch, historian, *For Fun and Profit: The Transformation of Leisure Into Consumption,* 1990

**Which of the following cultural and social shifts resulted most directly from the trends described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the expansion of consumer culture
the emergence of regional cultures
the rise of donations to universities
the concentration of wealth

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Thus is the problem of rich and poor to be solved. The laws of accumulation will be left free, the laws of distribution free. Individualism will continue, but the millionaire will be but a trustee for the poor, intrusted for a season with a great part of the increased wealth of the community, but administering it for the community far better than it could or would have done for itself. The best minds will thus have reached a stage in the development of the race in which it is clearly seen that there is no mode of disposing of surplus wealth creditable to thoughtful and earnest men into whose hands it flows save by using it year by year for the general good.”

>-Source: Andrew Carnegie, “Wealth,” *North American Review,* 1889

**Which of the following issues of the period was Carnegie most likely concerned with in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the social problems caused by poverty
the strikes organized by labor unions
the emergence of voluntary organizations
the consolidation of major businesses

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Thus is the problem of rich and poor to be solved. The laws of accumulation will be left free, the laws of distribution free. Individualism will continue, but the millionaire will be but a trustee for the poor, intrusted for a season with a great part of the increased wealth of the community, but administering it for the community far better than it could or would have done for itself. The best minds will thus have reached a stage in the development of the race in which it is clearly seen that there is no mode of disposing of surplus wealth creditable to thoughtful and earnest men into whose hands it flows save by using it year by year for the general good.”

>-Source: Andrew Carnegie, “Wealth,” *North American Review,* 1889

**Carnegie’s account in the excerpt above most directly encouraged which of the following changes in subsequent years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Several prominent business leaders began donating money to good causes.
Women organized voluntary associations to advocate for social reform.
Congress implemented several regulations to improve working conditions.
Business leaders began merging businesses to reduce the costs of production.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Thus is the problem of rich and poor to be solved. The laws of accumulation will be left free, the laws of distribution free. Individualism will continue, but the millionaire will be but a trustee for the poor, intrusted for a season with a great part of the increased wealth of the community, but administering it for the community far better than it could or would have done for itself. The best minds will thus have reached a stage in the development of the race in which it is clearly seen that there is no mode of disposing of surplus wealth creditable to thoughtful and earnest men into whose hands it flows save by using it year by year for the general good.”

>-Source: Andrew Carnegie, “Wealth,” *North American Review,* 1889

**The ideas about the responsibility of wealth expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Gospel of Wealth
Social Gospel
Social Darwinism
Americanization

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Where does the money go? Andrew Carnegie makes a specialty of public libraries in his gifts, with a good sprinkling of checks among educational institutions of established reputation. . . . John D. Rockefeller takes splendid care of the University of Chicago, and has several millions annually to pass around among Baptist institutions and other interests that appeal to his consideration. . . .

>“It is quite safe to assert that the majority of gifts and bequests goes to colleges and universities, with homes and hospitals for men, women, and children next, and memorial buildings and church edifices following. . . . Giving has become a business.”

>-Source: George J. Hagar, “Magnitude of American Benefactions,” *The Review of Reviews,* 1904

**The author’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise in philanthropic donations
the creation of a federal income tax
the emergence of a middle class
the improvements in standards of living

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Where does the money go? Andrew Carnegie makes a specialty of public libraries in his gifts, with a good sprinkling of checks among educational institutions of established reputation. . . . John D. Rockefeller takes splendid care of the University of Chicago, and has several millions annually to pass around among Baptist institutions and other interests that appeal to his consideration. . . .

>“It is quite safe to assert that the majority of gifts and bequests goes to colleges and universities, with homes and hospitals for men, women, and children next, and memorial buildings and church edifices following. . . . Giving has become a business.”

>-Source: George J. Hagar, “Magnitude of American Benefactions,” *The Review of Reviews,* 1904

**The author’s account in the excerpt above most directly reflects which of the following changes in the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Business leaders began giving to charity to help the less fortunate.
Voluntary organizations  formed to address social problems.
Businesses hired managers to cope with complex organizational needs.
Universities expanded as people sought new job opportunities.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“Where does the money go? Andrew Carnegie makes a specialty of public libraries in his gifts, with a good sprinkling of checks among educational institutions of established reputation. . . . John D. Rockefeller takes splendid care of the University of Chicago, and has several millions annually to pass around among Baptist institutions and other interests that appeal to his consideration. . . .

>“It is quite safe to assert that the majority of gifts and bequests goes to colleges and universities, with homes and hospitals for men, women, and children next, and memorial buildings and church edifices following. . . . Giving has become a business.”

>-Source: George J. Hagar, “Magnitude of American Benefactions,” *The Review of Reviews,* 1904

**The excerpt best serves as evidence of which of the following trends during the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the enhancement of educational opportunities as business leaders made philanthropic donations
the development of a distinctive middle class as new job opportunities opened up for highly-skilled workers
the influence of the socialist and utopian movements on social reforms meant to address the conditions of poverty
the rise in government regulation following the concentration of wealth and business consolidation
